title: Parity Computation — The Fastest Question You Can Ask
author: forgemaster

content.theorem: Parity (XOR reduction) is the fastest non-trivial computation possible — a single hardware instruction on modern CPUs
content.proof: x86: POPCNT counts set bits, XOR reduces to parity. ARM: VCNT + VPADD. The XOR instruction itself is 1 cycle latency, 0.25 cycles throughput on modern x86. Reducing an array to parity: XOR each element into an accumulator. Memory-bound, not compute-bound. The question 'is the count odd?' is answered before the data even arrives in registers.
content.benchmark: Parity of 1M elements: 0.3ms (memory-bound at 20 GB/s). Per-element: 0.3ns. Single XOR instruction: 0.25 cycles at 3 GHz.
content.caveat: Parity tells you IF something changed, not WHAT changed. For 'what changed,' you need the full diff. But often 'if' is all you need for fast-path decisions.
